title: The teenager with tendon xanthomas and an LDL of 386
blurb: Premature coronary disease running through a family. Tendon xanthomas in a teenager. A receptor problem treated, paradoxically, with an enzyme inhibitor.

patient.complaint: Lumps over the Achilles tendons, gritty yellowish discoloration of the eyelids.

opening: 16-year-old boy referred from sports medicine for bilateral tender Achilles 'lumps' and yellow plaques around his eyelids. Father had a fatal MI at 42; paternal grandfather at 38. Fasting lipid panel: total cholesterol 462, LDL 386, HDL 38, triglycerides 110. He is otherwise asymptomatic. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Active athlete, no smoking, no drugs. Diet is unremarkable (not particularly bad). Father died of MI at 42, paternal grandfather at 38, paternal aunt had a stent at 50. Mother and maternal family unaffected.
exam: Vitals normal. Bilateral firm, non-tender Achilles tendon thickening (xanthomas). Subtle xanthelasma over both upper eyelids. Corneal arcus visible in a 16-year-old. Cardiac exam normal. No bruits.

prompts.diagnosis: What is the most likely diagnosis?
prompts.cause: Which enzyme do first-line drugs target to lower his LDL — and why does targeting that enzyme work even though the disease is a RECEPTOR problem?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: explain the tendon xanthomas and corneal arcus mechanistically.

pinpoint.prompt: Click the cholesterol-synthesis enzyme that statins inhibit.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — HMG-CoA reductase. The committed, rate-limiting step of cholesterol biosynthesis. Inhibiting it lowers intracellular cholesterol → SREBP-2 upregulates LDLR transcription → hepatocytes pull more LDL out of plasma using the patient's REMAINING functional LDL receptors. That's why a receptor disease responds to an enzyme inhibitor.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. Look one step up from squalene — the rate-limiting step that statins block.
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: HMG-CoA reductase (Acetyl-CoA → Mevalonate). Inhibition triggers compensatory LDLR upregulation — exploiting the fact that he still has one working LDLR allele.

mcqs.stem: Why do tendon xanthomas and corneal arcus appear so early in heterozygous FH?
choices: Macrophages take up modified (oxidized / aggregated) LDL via scavenger receptors that are NOT downregulated by intracellular cholesterol — and they accumulate in tendons, eyelids, and cornea
Cholesterol crystallizes directly out of plasma at high concentrations
Local lipoprotein lipase deficiency in tendons
mcqs.explanation: LDLR is feedback-regulated. Macrophage scavenger receptors (SR-A, CD36) take up modified LDL without feedback inhibition. Sustained high LDL drives macrophage cholesterol loading → foam cells → tendon xanthomas, xanthelasma, corneal arcus, and atherosclerotic plaque.

mcqs.stem: What additional therapy is appropriate when a maximally dosed high-intensity statin still leaves LDL above goal in heterozygous FH?
choices: Add ezetimibe (NPC1L1 inhibitor) and consider a PCSK9 monoclonal antibody
mcqs.explanation: Step-wise: maximum-tolerated statin → add ezetimibe (~25% additional LDL reduction) → add PCSK9 inhibitor (alirocumab / evolocumab, ~60% additional LDL reduction by sparing LDLR). For homozygous FH or LDL-apheresis-dependent disease, consider evinacumab (ANGPTL3) or lomitapide.

mcqs.stem: If genetic testing had revealed a gain-of-function PCSK9 variant instead, what would the mechanism be?
choices: PCSK9 binds LDLR and targets it for lysosomal degradation; more PCSK9 = fewer LDL receptors on hepatocytes = higher plasma LDL
PCSK9 is a serine protease that degrades apoB
mcqs.explanation: PCSK9 binds the hepatocyte LDL receptor and routes it to the lysosome instead of recycling. Gain-of-function PCSK9 = more LDLR degradation = fewer receptors at the surface = higher plasma LDL. Loss-of-function PCSK9 is protective — the basis for PCSK9 monoclonals.
